text: Last Friday, the Dynamis community ran a 90-minute live workshop with Colm A. Dean on something most AI coding content gets wrong. The code base, 97 engineers in the room. Six strategies. This video is the quick summary. Here's the problem. Vibe coding doesn't scale. Every team starts simple. A clean greenfield. One cloud code session. Smooth shipping. But it always ends in 2,000 line god components. Agents hallucinating patterns you never showed them. Context bloat killing every session. And one specific failure mode is killing people. A bad prime command. Some teams run a prime. And it pulls in 50,000 tokens before they've written a single line of code. The agent's context is its most precious resource. Pre-bloat it and reasoning quality collapses. You're already losing. Here's the pivot. File size isn't the right metric. A 2,000 line file with three imports is fine. A 500 line file imported by 20 others is a fire. Coupling, not lines. Six strategies. Each one stands alone. Combined, they turn any messy code base into something an agent can actually navigate. One, vertical slice architecture. Organized by feature, not by layer. Two, brownfield day one. Use parallel subagents to derive the AI layer the first time. Three, context management. Three tiers. Four, focus priming. One prime command per slice. Five, parallel work trees. Multiple agents. Isolated copies. Zero collisions. Six, coupling overline count. Strategy one. Vertical slice architecture. Most code bases are organized by layer. Controllers in one folder. Services in another. Repositories in a third. Tests way over there. That's fine for humans who already have the system in their head. It's terrible for a coding agent. To touch one feature, the agent chases that feature across four folders, burning tokens at every hop. Vertical slice flips it. One folder per feature, owning everything that feature needs. Models, schemas, routes, service, errors, tests, all in one place. The agent loads the slice, has the full feature in context, ships. And here's the part most senior engineers get wrong. You don't refactor everything. Start with the next new feature. Build it in the vertical slice shape. Let the brownfield decay around it. Six months later, every new feature ships vertical slice, and you've quietly migrated. Strategy two. Most engineers conflate two different scenarios into one word, brownfield. Type A. There's no AI layer yet. The agent walks in blind. You do this once, ever, per code base. Spin up parallel explorer subagents. Five for small. Eight or 10 for a sprawling monorepo. Each one investigates one slice. They converge into a single code base analysis markdown. Then a second round of builder subagents derive your global rules, plus a customized command suite. 10 minutes. Done forever. Type B. The AI layer exists. New epic incoming. This is where you live 95% of the time. Every sprint reads the layer. Edits where reality has drifted. Ships. Loops back. And a question came up in the chat. How do you know if the AI layer is trustworthy? Run the fill gaps audit. Same parallel subagents pattern. Different goal. The agent compares what the layer claims against the actual code. Surfaces drift. Closes gaps. Strategy three. Context management. Every code base has three structurally different kinds of context. On demand. And layer two. Global is auto loaded every session. Text stack. Code style. Must know conventions. Keep this under 300 lines. On demand is loaded only when relevant. Pattern guides. Per feature readmes. Layer two is the plan for this specific ticket. Loaded once. Discarded after. If you put everything in global, you pay a token tax every session for context that's irrelevant 90% of the time. And another chat question. Markdown or HTML. Markdown wins. HTML burns tokens for layout you don't need. Strategy four. Focused priming. A vanilla prime command tries to read the whole project. On 50,000 lines, manageable. On a 500,000 line mono repo, you blow the context budget before the task starts. The fix is a family of prime commands. Each loads one slice. Prime server loads the back end. Prime client loads the front end. Prime components for shared UI. Prime endpoint for adding a new API route end to end. Each command loads five to ten files. The agent comes online with the right context for one type of task. And who writes these prime commands? You let the brownfield day one skill generate them. From your code base. Customized. Day one. And the workflow inside every ticket is the same three move loop. You plan. Vertical slice the work. Write the spec in its own session. Subagent scout. Then Claude implements. Fresh session. Only the plan loaded. Then you validate. Lint. Type check. Unit. End to end. Manual review. Five steps. Not eight. Not 12. Five is the ceiling. Anything passed that is over engineering. Strategy five. Parallel work trees. Same wallet, faster delivery. Get work trees give you in working directories from the same repo. Each on its own branch. Each isolated. Combined with multiple Claude code sessions. And you have in parallel agent extremes. Two terminals. Two tickets. Two pull requests. No crosstalk. People assume parallel sessions equal end times the cost. Not really. The total work is the same total tokens. You're compressing wall clock by running concurrently. The constraint isn't your spend. It's how fast you can review and merge. Most teams find their bottleneck is the human reviewer, not the agent. Strategy six. Component sizing by coupling, not by line count. The God component anti-pattern is what kills your AI workflow. Three questions. One. Can you work on this file in isolation? Or do you always need to also load three other files to make sense of it? Two. Does the agent have clear boundaries? Or does every change ripple outside the file? Three. Is the public API obvious? If any answer is no, the component is too coupled. Regardless of how many lines and finding and use code. AST grep. It walks the code base as an abstract syntax tree. Anything with no inbound references is dead. Pair it with your validate step. And here's the line that landed hardest. From Anthropic's own article on Claude code and large code bases. Subagents are used to split exploration from editing. Your primary session edits files. Subagents take the context hit. The most precious resource when you're working with a coding agent isn't your wallet. It's the context. Protect it like a finite tank. So which one are you running today? Vertical slices or shared test folders? Drop your take in the comments. And then go run the brownfield day one skill on your code base this weekend. 10 minutes. You'll know more about your own repo than you do right now. If you want to learn more about AI, check out the dynamos.ai community.
